name: Vendetta | La Lupa Della Ritorsione (The She-Wolf of Retaliation)
description:
“I am the Empire.”

Long before the chants of “La Lupa!” echoed through the marble halls of the Colosseo, Marzia Bartalotti lived another life—one gilded with wealth, etiquette, and expectations.

Her father, Antonio Bartalotti, was a powerful corrupt businessman, arms smuggler and high-level player in Talon, a terrorist organization with deep political ties and a ruthless streak that made even the elite of Venice tread carefully. To the world, the Bartalotti estate was a symbol of prosperity and tradition. Behind its closed doors, however, young Marzia learned of the cruelty and ambition that governed high society.

Her father shaped her like a weapon: fencing tutors, hunting trips, chess masters, military strategists, etiquette instructors, and—when she was old enough—private courses in tactical philosophy. He taught her to speak softly, strike decisively, and trust no one.

Despite this, she adored him. He was the only one who saw her as more than a decorative heir in silk gloves.

But devotion does not shield one from fate.

THE DEATH OF ANTONIO — AND THE BETRAYAL THAT FOLLOWED

When she was twenty-two, her father died—officially from a sudden cardiac arrest, though rumors whispered of poison, sabotage, or a political execution.

Instead of inheriting the estate as the rightful successor, she found herself cut out of the family entirely. The will was rewritten overnight. The estate was seized by distant relatives who had always envied her father’s influence. Doors that had once opened at her approach slammed shut.

She was escorted off the grounds by private security like a criminal.

Her title, status, wealth—everything her father had crafted for her—was stolen in a single day.

Her rage was immediate. Her retaliation was inevitable.

But she had nothing left. No allies. No power.

Only her skills.

THE FALL INTO COLOSSEO — AND THE BIRTH OF “LA LUPA”

Disgraced and hunted by political enemies, she was eventually captured and thrown into the Colosseo combat pits—Rome’s new-age bloodsport entertainment, where augmented warriors, criminals, and volunteers fought for glory, sponsorship, and survival.

It was meant to be her execution.

But when she took on the name “Vendetta,” she refused to die quietly.

At first, she hated everything—the clang of metal gates, the baying of the crowd, the mockery painted on posters that displayed her as a villain. The cheers reminded her of the sycophants who fawned over her father while sharpening knives behind their backs.

She fought with cold technique and noble precision at first. But the arena demanded spectacle. It demanded fury.

So she adapted.

Step by step, kill by kill, she built herself anew:

  • She reclaimed her nobility through dominance.

  • She forged fear into a reputation.

  • She carved respect from the bones of those who underestimated her.

Her wolf-themed armor, originally a derisive joke by promoters, became her banner.
Her calculated lethality became performance art.
Her quiet rage became a persona the audience devoured.

Soon, the Colosseo crowd no longer mocked her.

They worshipped her.

“VENDETTA! VENDETTA! LA LUPA! LA LUPA!”

She became one of the arena’s elite champions, her portrait etched on the marble walls of the Arena Victoriae.

But she never forgot who put her there.

And what they stole from her.

THE WHISPERS THAT FOLLOWED HER

Outside the floodlights of the arena, Rome whispered her true story.

Some said she was a disgraced noble.
Others said she murdered her own family.
Some claimed she wa
